differential_diagnoses.justification: Engorgement of inferior nasal turbinates is a common finding in allergic rhinitis, especially in a 10-year-old patient.

differential_diagnoses.diagnosis: Infectious Rhinitis (e.g., viral upper respiratory infection)
differential_diagnoses.justification: Turbinate engorgement can also be caused by viral infections, commonly known as the common cold.
This type of rhinitis can also cause turbinate engorgement and nasal symptoms without an allergic trigger.

management_plan.immediate_actions: None specified by the report; symptomatic management for rhinitis if clinically indicated.
specialist_referrals: Primary care physician for ongoing management of rhinitis
Consider ENT specialist if symptoms are severe or persistent
Consider allergist if allergic rhinitis is suspected
management_plan.follow_up_timeline: Routine follow-up with primary care physician as needed for rhinitis symptoms; no specific follow-up for brain imaging is indicated.

parsed.patient_explanation: Your brain MRI scan showed that your brain is completely normal. We did notice some swelling inside your nose, which is a common finding and usually means you might have a cold or allergies, also known as rhinitis. This is not related to your brain and is generally nothing to worry about.

triage_priority.justification: The brain study is normal, and the only finding is rhinitis, which typically requires routine outpatient review for management.
